source.title: The Cerebellum: Adaptive Prediction for Movement and Cognition

confidence_rationale: This review provides a quantitative timescale claim: the cerebellum's predictive capabilities span 'relatively short time intervals relevant for skilled movement (up to a second or so)' and the paper explicitly characterises temporal precision as a key distinguishing feature of cerebellar processing vs. cerebral cortex. The cerebellum is described as operating under tight temporal constraints, functioning best for continuous, short-horizon prediction. This weakens MECH-070's claim that E2 (cerebellar analogue) has a longer planning horizon than E1 (cortical), but the confidence is modest because the paper also notes cerebellar involvement in sequencing and cognitive domains, suggesting partial extension beyond pure 1-step prediction.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Cerebellar predictive capabilities span relatively short time intervals relevant for skilled movement (up to a second or so); temporal precision is a key characteristic differentiating cerebellar processing from cerebral cortex; the cerebellum operates under tight temporal constraints.
mapping.ree_translation: E2's natural prediction horizon, if it faithfully implements a cerebellar forward model, would be bounded to short intervals (sub-second biological equivalent). E1 as cortical slow predictor (LSTM, prediction_horizon=20 steps) represents the longer-horizon system. MECH-070's direction claim is inverted relative to this biological characterisation.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The 'up to a second or so' claim is in biological time; the mapping to discrete grid-world steps is non-trivial. One REE step could correspond to much less than a second. The paper also notes cerebellar involvement in sequencing (e.g., language, cognitive tasks), which might involve longer effective horizons, but these are chains of short predictions, not single long-horizon rollouts.
mapping.source_context: 2017 Trends in Cognitive Sciences review, published in a high-impact journal. PMC open access. Explicitly quantifies the cerebellar timescale constraint, which is rarer than qualitative characterisations.

confidence_components.notes: High quality source (Trends in Cognitive Sciences, Popa et al. group has strong cerebellar electrophysiology background). Mapping fidelity lower because the quantitative timescale (up to ~1 second) does not map cleanly to REE's discrete rollout_horizon parameter. Transfer risk: the biological temporal constraint is on continuous prediction; REE's failure mode may be different in kind.
